disease.name: Dubin-Johnson Syndrome
disease.note: Benign autosomal recessive disorder causing conjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to deficient biliary excretion of conjugated bilirubin. Caused by ABCC2 gene mutation affecting MRP2 (canalicular multispecific organic anion transporter). Black liver pigmentation on biopsy. Excellent prognosis; no treatment required. Typically presents in adolescence or young adulthood with isolated elevated conjugated bilirubin.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: All cases are benign; no emergency. Abnormal LFTs or ALP suggest alternative diagnosis.

historyFeatures.note: Mild conjugated hyperbilirubinemia; fluctuates in severity
Conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble and appears in urine
OCPs may exacerbate jaundice in women
Important: pruritus is NOT a feature

examFeatures.note: Classic finding: dark black-brown liver from pigment accumulation

laboratory.name: Total and direct (conjugated) bilirubin
laboratory.indication: elevated total bilirubin (typically 2-5 mg/dL), direct fraction >50%; hallmark

laboratory.name: LFTs (ALT, AST, ALP, GGT)
laboratory.indication: normal in Dubin-Johnson; any elevation suggests alternative diagnosis

laboratory.name: Urine coproporphyrin profile
laboratory.indication: diagnostic: total urine coproporphyrin normal but coproporphyrin I (isomer I) >80% (normal <30%)

laboratory.name: Oral cholecystogram (historic)
laboratory.indication: non-visualization of gallbladder even though bile ducts are patent; not used anymore

laboratory.name: Liver biopsy
laboratory.indication: gross: black liver; histology: coarse dark pigment (epinephrine metabolites) in hepatocyte lysosomes, predominantly centrilobular; normal architecture; no fibrosis or inflammation

imaging.name: Abdominal ultrasound
imaging.indication: normal bile ducts; normal liver echotexture; rule out obstruction

pathways.criteria: Confirmed Dubin-Johnson syndrome, any bilirubin level
pathways.treatment: Reassure patient that Dubin-Johnson syndrome is benign and requires no treatment. No dietary restrictions. No activity limitations. No medications needed. Avoid unnecessary investigations for isolated conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Inform patient that jaundice may be exacerbated by OCPs, pregnancy, and intercurrent illness — this is expected and benign. No risk of cirrhosis or liver failure. Normal life expectancy. Genetic counseling if desired.
